explicit_text: When I first came here, Dean Wolin told me that Immokalee was an old Seminole camping ground, as noted in tourist literature. Some thought it was just a cattle grazing area started by settlers from Fort Myers, but after talking with Captain J. F. Jaudon and Mrs. Platt, I believe the Indian origin story is right. I hope to visit Immokalee after January 1st to learn more about its history and connection to the Seminole Indians and early Florida days.

explicit_text: Dean Wolin was a pioneer and United States Commissioner to the Seminole Indians who first told me that Immokalee was an old Seminole camping ground. His insight sparked my interest in the area's history, even though some local sources disagreed with him. His role helped guide my investigations into the Seminole presence and early settlements in Lee County, Florida.

His position gave him authority to speak about places like Immokalee being old Seminole camping grounds. This role was important in preserving and interpreting Seminole heritage during the late 19th and early 20th centuries.

explicit_text: Lake County, Florida, is where I visited a pioneer home with Mrs. Platt and her family. The house there, typical of the rural southland, has withstood three hurricanes and holds many memories of early Florida life. The area’s history and the stories of its residents like Mary Brennan provide rich insight into the region’s past.

explicit_text: My husband John Burrell grafted many hibiscus and citrus trees in the Allapattah section of Miami, creating unusual combinations that still exist. The garden there was once part of a rich marl prairie that was formerly the waterway of the old Allapattah River, now known as Reybold's Canal. This area holds a special place in our family history and Florida’s agricultural heritage.

explicit_text: I am Mary Brennan, a seventy-eight-year-old pioneer woman who has lived through many changes in Florida. My family, the Brennans, came from South Carolina and originally from England, settling in northern Florida and Georgia counties. I remember the days before the Civil War, the time of slavery, and the hardships of the Reconstruction era. My husband John and I have worked hard in citrus and gardening, and I cherish the old traditions and stories of our family and community.

explicit_text: The Brennan family, originally from South Carolina and England, settled in northern Florida and parts of Georgia like Madison, Brooks, and Thomas Counties. We were slave owners before the Civil War and managed large plantations growing cotton, sugarcane, and other crops. Our family history is deeply tied to the agricultural and social history of the South, including the difficult Reconstruction era.

explicit_text: The Creek people were Native Americans living in northern Florida and southern Georgia who gathered wild pecans as an important part of their winter food supply. Their presence and practices were part of the natural and cultural landscape before and during the early days of our family’s settlement. Understanding their role helps me appreciate the region's indigenous history.

explicit_text: The American Civil War was a difficult time for my family and the South, lasting four years and bringing hardship to plantations and farms. Before the war, we had slaves and plenty of meat and crops, but the war disrupted everything. The aftermath, especially during Reconstruction, was even harder as many people struggled to rebuild.

explicit_text: The Reconstruction era was a tough period after the Civil War when carpetbaggers took control and many farms and plantations fell into ruin. There were no slaves to work the land, and many people couldn't feed their livestock. This era marked a significant decline in the quality of native cattle and pigs in Florida and was a time of great struggle for my family and community.

explicit_text: During the Reconstruction era after the Civil War, carpetbaggers took control of the South, including northern Florida where my family lived. Their governance contributed to the ruin of many farms and plantations, as there was no longer slave labor and many people were unable to maintain their land. This period was marked by hardship and loss for families like mine.

This period was marked by hardship and loss for families like mine.

explicit_text: Though not directly mentioned in my family stories, Harriet Beecher Stowe was known for her book 'Uncle Tom's Cabin,' which depicted slavery. Her character Simon Legree symbolizes the harshness of slave owners, contrasting with the more neighborly relationships my family had with their slaves. Her work influenced many people's views on slavery during and after my family's time.

While my family owned slaves, our relationships were more neighborly and peaceful, unlike the harshness Legree symbolizes. His character helps illustrate the different experiences of slavery in the South.

Their presence marked a difficult time for Southern families like mine.
